narrative:
Isolated late-day thundershowers Tuesday were a sampler for what arrives today. We have early sunshine and warming temperatures this morning. Clouds develop later this morning and continue into this evening. Showers and thunderstorms develop by early afternoon and remain in play through early evening. Isolated heavy rain and even small hail are possible.

Thursday through Saturday, afternoon showers and thundershowers are less active but still possible. A front moves through Easter Sunday with a period of showers and thundershowers.

Today, we begin the RaysWeather Appalachian Fog Registry. With fog developing early Thursday morning, we launch our new program to name fog events and help communicate driving hazards. The fog that develops late tonight into early Thursday is named “Arlis.” We track Arlis Thursday morning.

Check out the April 2026 Forecast Outlook.

The "Mix and Match" Special Offer continues on all the Snowman O'Mometer Pint Glass and Mug (or you can long for a 5 Golfball Day with that pint glass as well). Buy one, and get another drinkware item at 40% off.

Get the free RWC Mobile App...: Apple and Android

Hi: 73

Showers and thunderstorms develop by early afternoon and remain in play through early evening. Isolated heavy rain and even small hail are possible. Thursday through Saturday, afternoon showers and thundershowers are less active but still possible. A front moves through Easter Sunday with a period of showers and thundershowers. Subscribe Forecast Discussion The sequence of weather events from today through Sunday is not in question, but the devil is in the details. A Bermuda High provides a warm, moist southerly flow through the rest of the week. A cold front sweeps through Easter Sunday. It is clear that this afternoon is the most active period of the week for showers and thunderstorms. After early sun warms things up, the convective stage is set for showers and thunderstorms starting early in the afternoon. Be alert for strong storms in the region, with isolated heavy rain and even small hail. Showers and thunderstorms remain in play into the early evening. Expect fog to start the day Thursday. Sunshine then warms things again, with isolated afternoon showers and thundershowers. Friday and Saturday continue that theme, with a bit more emphasis on Friday for afternoon thundershower coverage. A front moves through Sunday. Some model guidance is 3–4 hours quicker with the onset of showers, getting things started before sunrise and ending precipitation by afternoon. Others have showers and thundershowers developing after sunrise and continuing into the afternoon. We’ll have more clarity as we get closer. After the front passes through, we get a significant cooldown, but computer guidance differs on how cool. Our forecast splits the difference for now. Again, we’ll refine the details as we get closer. Mostly clear, dry weather is expected Monday and Tuesday (and Wednesday). Milder weather follows. Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters. All Photos Submit Photo Latest Insights April 2026 Outlook Monthly Forecast Southern Appalachians – SW Virginia & Western North Carolina The April Pattern: Bumpy Road toward Summer Spring in the Southern Appalachians is often characterized by teasing warmth and chilly disappointments, but ultimately, we are progressing toward warmer weather. This April will fit that mold very well. March and April tend to be wetter than the winter months, but not as wet as May through August. And frost… For NW NC and SW VA, “don’t plant until Mother’s Day” is always the best advice. SW NC and the Foothills are typically safe for planting about a week or so before Mother’s Day. The NE Foothills are usually a bit earlier still. Snow? The Foothills are typically “done” by April, but the mountain region can still have an anomalous snow event. It’s worth noting that the biggest snow event ever recorded in NC occurred atop Mt. Pisgah in the first week of May 1992. Bottom Line for April 2026 Temperatures will average near normal for the month, but with numerous “ups” and “downs” in a fast westerly flow. Precipitation is expected to be near average as well, with a core of wetter weather to our west from Texas to the Ohio Valley. If we reach average rainfall, it will come from the frequency of rain events, probably not from a few heavy events. While stormy weather will occur as we move toward warmer weather, we do not expect a particularly stormy month. April Normals for Various Locations in the Southern Appalachians (Temperatures in degrees F, Precipitation and Snow in inches)
Location Avg High Avg Low Avg Precip Avg Snow
Asheville 68.9 45.8 3.69 0.3
Asheville Airport 69.4 44.6 4.17 0.2
Banner Elk 60.4 38.6 4.83 0.8
Beech Mtn 53.3 36.4 4.92 4.4
Boone 61.7 36.9 5.52 1.0
Burnsville 66.0 41.9 4.21 0.5
Galax 64.7 39.4 4.07 0.5
Hendersonville 68.3 42.8 4.84 0.0
Hickory 70.0 47.7 4.08 0.0
Jefferson 63.1 36.3 4.78 0.0
Lenoir 70.1 45.0 4.44 0.0
Marion 69.8 45.9 4.90 0.0
Marshall 68.8 38.3 3.71 0.8
Morganton 70.6 44.9 4.29 0.0
Mt. Mitchell 50.2 32.0 6.52 7.1
North Wilkesboro 69.9 43.6 4.72 0.0
Sparta 63.4 39.8 5.36 1.0
Waynesville 65.6 39.6 4.54 0.4
Wytheville 64.5 39.2 3.75 0.5
Temperatures: Near Average but Variable The overall pattern for April features a progressive flow across the United States, with a mean trough positioned to our west and later to our north—centered from the northern Plains into eastern Canada—and a ridge anchored across the southern U.S. That places the Southern Appalachians in between, in a corridor of fast-moving west-to-east flow. In this setup, systems do not stall. Instead, fronts move through every few days, bringing a warm first few days followed by alternating periods of warmth ahead of each system and cooler air behind it. The pattern does not lock into either a warm or cold regime, but keeps the weather changing regularly through the month. The following 7-day 500 mb height map illustrates a common pattern during the month. It shows a broad, relatively flat west-to-east flow across the United States, with no strong blocking in place. The Southern Appalachians sit between cooler air to the north and warmer air to the south, with systems moving steadily along this boundary. This setup favors frequent changes in air masses rather than a prolonged warm or cold stretch, which is why temperatures trend near average overall but swing up and down every few days. Precipitation: Frequent, not Prolonged Rain The progressive pattern brings precipitation in frequent periods rather than prolonged ones. Fronts move through every few days, each bringing a period of rain followed by a break. Systems don’t stall, so extended multi-day rain is unlikely, but the pattern also doesn’t shut down, so long dry stretches are unlikely. Overall, rainfall is driven by frequency rather than intensity, supporting near-normal totals. Western sections of the Appalachians may receive more rain, with totals decreasing eastward into the Foothills. April Outlook at a Glance
Week Temperature Precipitation
Week 1 (Apr 1–7) Above average but turning cooler toward the end of the period Near average
Week 2 (Apr 8–14) Near to slightly above average Near average
Week 3 (Apr 15–21) Near average Near average
Week 4 (Apr 22–30) Slightly cooler than average Near average
Overall for April Near average Near average
Factors Considered in this Forecast Several large-scale climate factors contributed to these forecast decisions: AO (Arctic Oscillation): Expected to remain near neutral, not favoring a deeper trough in the Eastern U.S. NAO (North Atlantic Oscillation): Near neutral to slightly positive, suggesting no prolonged deep trough in the East PNA (Pacific–North American): Negative, favoring a trough in the Western U.S. and ridging in the East ENSO: Recently turned neutral and trending toward El Niño this summer. This is a weak signal for April, so greater weight is placed on modeling and North American indices. Looking further down the road, El Niño later in the summer would mean a less active hurricane season in the Atlantic. Extended ECMWF, GFS, and CMC modeling: Overall pattern agreement is good, though differences exist. The ECMWF is drier than the GFS and Canadian. The GFS is slightly cooler than the ECMWF and Canadian. All show a Western U.S. trough and an East Coast ridge early in the month, evolving toward a trough over eastern Canada and the Northeast. The NOAA long-range forecast calls for slightly above-average precipitation and warmer-than-average temperatures. It was not a factor in this forecast. Mar 30, 2026 Monday Message for March 30, 2026 After a chilly last weekend in March, it's back to full-on springtime conditions for western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia. We discuss the Easter Week pattern shift on this week's Monday Message. Mar 30, 2026 East vs West: An Explainer Mountains Make Weather One of the most common questions we get on a weekly basis at Ray’s Weather is not a strictly weather-adjacent one! If you are a long-time reader of Ray’s, you have seen countless references to a few prominent geographic features of our region: the Western Escarpment, the Eastern Escarpment, and the Blue Ridge. These features are very important for many local weather phenomena, and often act as sharp dividing lines between different types of weather simultaneously. You may well know what those things are, but if that isn’t the case for you, we are here to clear the air on what we mean when we reference those places. First up… The Western Escarpment Before we even get into the specifics of where this region lies, we have to define what an “escarpment” really is. Merriam-Webster offers: "A long cliff or steep slope separating two comparatively level or more gently sloping surfaces and resulting from erosion or faulting." Quite simply, the escarpment is the dividing region between foothills and mountains. It is a broader area that encompasses where the foothills begin to rise more dramatically up to higher terrain. The Western Escarpment region runs along the base of the Smoky Mountains, following the Tennessee line to Madison County, before things get a bit muddier between there and Ashe County. It generally hugs the Tennessee line in Yancey and Mitchell Counties to Roan Mountain, but then dips into North Carolina more, with Beech and Sugar Mountain lying just to the east of the Western Escarpment region. After that, it cuts north past Snake Mountain and Pond Mountain, before curving eastward around the Grayson Highlands. The Eastern Escarpment This one is a bit easier to see and define. The dividing line between mountain and foothill is more pronounced on the eastern slopes of the Southern Appalachians. The Eastern Escarpment parallels the Blue Ridge itself, running through far northern South Carolina, before cutting north through Chimney Rock, through McDowell County to Linville Gorge, staying east of Grandfather Mountain and The Blowing Rock, riding the county lines northeastward to the Virginia border. From there, it snakes up to Bent Mountain and Roanoke as it leaves the Ray’s Weather region. Places like Lake James, Edgemont, and Stone Mountain State Park are within the Eastern Escarpment region. Not in the mountains, but right up against them. The Blue Ridge Lastly, the thin blue line that parallels the Eastern Escarpment is what we know as “The Blue Ridge.” This one may have some disagreement associated with it, as the entire mountain range in North Carolina is often referred to as “The Blue Ridge Mountains.” In our case, we are referring to a specific, semi-continuous ridgeline that marks the final, eastern frontier of the higher terrain, before dropping down into the escarpment region. The easiest way to see what we mean is to take a ride on the Blue Ridge Parkway from just east of Mount Mitchell all the way up to Roanoke. The Parkway essentially rides right along the Blue Ridge in those areas. For example, if you are standing on Grandfather Mountain and looking east, you are along the crest of the Blue Ridge and gazing into the Eastern Escarpment region. Why These Features Matter Both escarpments and the Blue Ridge itself are very important dividing lines and focal points for several different genres of weather in the Southern Appalachians. The Western Escarpment is often referred to in the context of our snow events. When cold fronts blow down from the northwest, they send air rising up the higher terrain near the North Carolina/Tennessee border, producing the snow showers that favor west over east time and time again. It’s why Blowing Rock will never get as much snow as Beech Mountain in a northwest flow event: it isn’t just colder up top, there is also better access to air being forced up the mountains to produce precipitation. Meanwhile, on the Eastern Escarpment, you often hear us use the words “wedge” or “cold-air damming” to describe situations where cold air works its way down the east side of the Appalachians and often delivers gray, misty conditions to the Foothills and eastern mountains. The Eastern Escarpment and Blue Ridge are both important for cold-air damming (or CAD) events, as the cold air layer is so shallow that it is unable to work over the Blue Ridge. Areas closer to the Western Escarpment can experience mild, sunny days while Hickory and Wilkesboro fail to leave the 30s and 40s under cloudy skies. Sometimes, you can find a spot along the Blue Ridge to view the fog and clouds beneath you out over the Foothills! Fog over the Piedmont, viewed from western Polk County (Image: Evan Fisher) The Blue Ridge can also act in a similar fashion to the Western Escarpment during periods of moist southerly or southeasterly flow. The terrain forces the air upwards, and more clouds and precipitation is produced in a region of local enhancement. This was the case to an extreme degree during Helene, as exceptionally moist air rushed up from the south and southeast, resulting in the incredible, devastating rainfall totals of up to 30+ inches that were observed during that storm. These are certainly not the only major weather patterns that are influenced by the local terrain, but they are some of the most commonly affiliated with the escarpments and Blue Ridge. Lastly, we often refer to a variety of peaks in the Southern Appalachians. The map below gives you an excellent reference point for where your town lies in relation to the most notable peaks of our area.
